title: Dog Sakura
description: Adachi gets jealous of a pug. Shimamura decides to treat her like a dog for the day—and Adachi, true to form, goes along with it.
content:
By some twist of fate, I grew up as someone who really loves dogs. A neighbor nearby owns a pug, and every time I see it on the street, I can't help stopping to greet it and shower it with affection. Despite not being the color of a chestnut, its name is 'Chestnut.' It's old now, its legs aren't too strong, so when it's taken out, it rides around in a baby stroller. On good days it walks a little on the way home, but today it sat quietly in the stroller the whole time.

Even so, whenever it wants a walk, it barks with surprising energy—you'd never guess its true condition from the sound alone.

While I was chatting peacefully with Chestnut and the elderly man who walked it, someone stood off to the side looking very, very displeased.

That someone was Adachi.

She'd asked to come over after school, so she was walking home with me now. Did Adachi not like dogs…? No—there was no 'did.' I already knew the answer. After waving goodbye to the old man and his old dog, I let out two mischievous little 'hehe' laughs.

We've been together long enough that I can guess what's going on in Adachi's head with almost perfect accuracy.

'Are you seriously jealous… of a pug, little Ada-chan?'

I revealed her thoughts lightly, half teasing. She immediately stammered, 'N-no! T-that's not it at all!'

'Really?'

Ah. So she is jealous.

Her head lowered, and her voice shrank into something faint and uncertain. But the tiny pout on her lips gave it all away.

'It's just… the face you made earlier… it was really gentle.'

Her voice trembled softly, like the warmth in it was trying to cling to me.

So yes—I'd guessed exactly right.

'Did I really make a face like that?'

I tugged at my own cheek. If I could see my 'gentle face,' I'd probably feel pretty proud. If I knew what expression it was, I might even wear it all the time.

If I told her I had a special affection for dogs, she'd overthink herself into exhaustion.

'Wait—are you saying I'm not gentle the rest of the time?'

I put on a shocked expression. Honestly, I've never thought of myself as gentle. Maybe only my grandma ever called me that. But since Adachi said it… maybe I should try to accept it.

…Later. Maybe later.

'Y-you're r-really… gentle…' Adachi insisted, her voice wobbling.

…Am I really that cold normally?

I honestly couldn't tell whether I should be reflecting on that.

Anyway: the current issue was that I'd been gentle with a dog but not gentle enough with Adachi. So, fine—I'd fix that.

But copying the affection I show dogs straight onto Adachi… yeah, that would be rough.

So I decided to flip it instead. Perfect.

'Then today, Adachi, you can be my dog.' Fastest solution possible. Probably.

'………………………………'

Adachi froze.

'………………………………'

And the moment the words left my mouth, I realized how terribly wrong they sounded.

But spilled words are spilled water.

'Good girl~ Be a good doggy for me, Ada~'

I used the same tone I'd used with the pug and patted her head. Adachi puffed up in protest, but never pulled away.

'This… feels kind of wrong.'

''Kind of'?' I couldn't help a wry smile. It was extremely wrong. If I told her to act more like a dog, she'd probably obey.

A 'Dog Adachi' definitely couldn't be seen in public. I needed to get her inside my house before anything escalated. Since I didn't have a leash, I grabbed her hand and tugged her along.

'A-and… um… th-that dog earlier and me… which one… d-do you like more?'

Stumbling behind me, she asked the most girlfriend-like jealous question possible.

Well—she is my girlfriend.

'They're not the same kind of liking. You can't compare them.'

'…What do you mean?'

'Like… my liking for rice crackers and my liking for Adachi. Totally different.'

'Liking' is shaped by desire.

My desire toward rice crackers: they're delicious and endlessly appealing.

My desire toward Adachi, though… um…

'…Are you saying you like dogs more than me?'

'No! Absolutely not, Ada-chan!'

I'd ruined the metaphor so badly she was now completely lost.

'Then… the kind of liking you feel for me… what kind is it?'

Her cheeks flushed as she asked.

I wondered when she'd be able to say 'like' without tripping over it.

'Well… let me think.'

My desire toward Adachi.

We've been together so often lately that I hadn't really put it into words.

If I had to choose one…

'Probably… destiny.'

I borrowed that word from a certain little one.

After meeting Adachi, I changed—at least a bit. And Adachi changed far too much after meeting me. Our meeting was like dropping a small stone into a still pond, sending endless ripples outward.

So calling it 'destiny' felt right. But since I didn't explain all that, she probably only understood half.

'Shimamura… the things you say are really hard to understand.'

'Are they?'

'Because… the way I like you… there's only one kind. So I'll never get it wrong.'

Adachi looked straight into my eyes—so earnest, so unwavering—that all I could do was accept it. We were almost home, yet here she was, tossing out a burning confession like it was nothing. I wanted to laugh awkwardly and flee.

But that's Adachi. Her feelings are simple and direct, like a wave rolling straight from the horizon—ready to swallow me whole.

'…Yeah. Maybe 'liking' really should be that simple.'

The simpler it is, the more easily it reaches the other person.

And when it reaches them clearly, nothing gets lost, hidden, or softened. If it's rejected, everything shatters—but Adachi still charges straight in.

That courage of hers is something I truly respect—especially as someone who always circles around things.

'All right, let's go, Adachi.' I tugged her hand like pulling a leash.

'Good girl, Adachi~ Let's go, Adachi~'

'…What is this…'

She was completely overwhelmed by my dog-owner act. Was I really being that dog-like? I didn't think I'd gone that far…

Things like 'shake' or 'sit' would cross some internal line as a dog lover. And besides, I knew with absolute certainty: if I asked, she would do it.

It looked like today I'd be spending time with Dog Adachi. Driven by a strange impulse, I quickened my pace, hoping momentum might pull me into being just a little more straightforward, too.

Then, just as we stepped into the house—Adachi seemed to gather her courage at last.

She barked.

'Woof.'
